Chapter 4: Three Major Classes of Chemical Reactions
4.1 Solution Concentration and the Role of Water as a    Solvent.
4.2 Precipitation Reactions.
4.3 Acid-Base Reactions.
4.4 Oxidation-Reduction (Redox) Reactions.
4.5 Elements in Redox Reactions.
4.6 The Reversibility of Reactions and the Equilibrium State.

Solution Concentration and the Role of Water as a Solvent
A solution is a homogenous mixture with a uniform composition and no visible boundaries among the components.
A solution consists of at least one solute and a solvent.
The solute is the substance that dissolves and is usually the smaller quantity.
The solvent is the substance in which the solute dissolves and is usually the larger quantity. Water is the solvent in an aqueous solution.
A particular solution has a specific concentration —the quantity of solute dissolved in a given quantity of solution (or of solvent).

Concentration of Solutions & Molarity
Many reactions occur in solution.
A solution consists of one or more solutes dissolved in a solvent .
The concentration of a solution is given by the quantity of solute present in a given quantity of solution.
Molarity ( M ) is often used to express concentration

Sample Problem 4.1: Problem and Plan
Calculating the Molarity of a Solution
PROBLEM: What is the molarity of an aqueous solution that contains 53.7 g of glycine (C 2 H 5 N O 2 ) in 495 mL of solution?
PLAN: Molarity is the number of moles of solute per liter of solution. Convert mass of glycine to amount (mol) by dividing by the molar mass. Divide moles by volume in liters.

Sample Problem 4.3: Problem and Plan
Preparing a Dilute Solution from a Concentrated Solution
PROBLEM: Isotonic saline is a 0.15 M aqueous solution of N a C l. How would you prepare 0.80 L of isotonic saline from a 6.0 M stock solution?
PLAN: To dilute a concentrated solution, we add only solvent, so the moles of solute are the same in both solutions. We use the volume and molarity of the dilute solution to calculate the moles of solute. Then we calculate the volume of concentrated  solution that contains the same number of moles.

The Polar Nature of Water
On the atomic scale, water’s great solvent power arises from the uneven distribution of electron charge and its bent molecular shape, which create a polar molecule.

Electron Distribution in Molecules of H 2 and H 2 O

Precipitation Reactions
In a precipitation reaction two soluble ionic compounds react to give an insoluble product, called a precipitate .
The precipitate forms through the net removal of ions from solution.
It is possible for more than one precipitate to form in such a reaction.
Three types of equations are used to represent aqueous reactions such as precipitation reactions.

Writing Equations for Aqueous Ionic Reactions
Figure 4.7
Access the text alternative for slide images. [slide112.xml]
Source: Stephen Frisch/McGraw Hill

Molecular Equations
The molecular equation reveals the least about the species that are actually in solution because it shows all reactants and products as if they were intact, undissociated compounds.

Total Ionic Equation
The total ionic equation shows all soluble ionic substances dissociated into ions. This gives the most accurate information about species in solution.
Spectator ions are ions that are not involved in the actual chemical change. Spectator ions appear unchanged on both sides of the total ionic equation.

Net Ionic Equation
The net ionic equation eliminates the spectator ions and shows only the actual chemical change .
Access the text alternative for slide images. [slide113.xml]
Source: Stephen Frisch/McGraw Hill

Predicting Whether a Precipitate Will Form
Note the ions present in the reactants.
Consider all possible cation-anion combinations.
Use the solubility rules to decide whether any of the ion combinations is insoluble.
Any insoluble combination identifies a precipitate that will form.

Solubility Rules for Ionic Compounds in Water
Table 4.1

Precipitation of PbI 2 , a Metathesis Reaction
Figure 4.8
Precipitation reactions are also called double displacement reactions or metathesis reactions.
Ions exchange partners and a precipitate forms.
Source: Stephen Frisch/McGraw Hill

Acid-Base Reactions
An acid is a substance that produces H ⁺ ions when dissolved in H 2 O.
A base is a substance that produces
ions when dissolved
in H 2 O.
An acid-base reaction is also called a neutralization reaction.

H ⁺ Ion as a Solvated Hydronium Ion
H ⁺ interacts strongly with H 2 O, forming H 3 O ⁺ in aqueous solution.
Figure 4.10

Acids and Bases as Electrolytes 1
Strong acids and strong bases dissociate completely into ions in aqueous solution.
They are strong electrolytes and conduct well in solution.
Figure 4.11
Source: Stephen Frisch/McGraw Hill

Acids and Bases as Electrolytes 2
Weak acids and weak bases dissociate very little into ions in aqueous solution.
They are weak electrolytes and conduct poorly in solution.
Figure 4.11
Source: Stephen Frisch/McGraw Hill

Classification of Soluble Compounds as Electrolytes
The table shows the classification of soluble compounds as electrolytes. The strong electrolytes are ionic compounds, strong acids and strong bases. The weak electrolytes are weak acids and weak bases. The nonelectrolytes are covalent (molecular) compounds.
Strong Electrolytes | Weak Electrolytes | Nonelectrolytes
Ionic compounds | Weak acids | Covalent (molecular) compounds
Strong acids | Weak bases
Strong bases
Table 4.3

Acid-Base Titrations
In a titration , the concentration of one solution is used to determine the concentration of another.
In an acid-base titration, a standard solution of base is usually added to a sample of acid of unknown molarity.
An acid-base indicator has different colors in acid and base, and is used to monitor the reaction progress.
At the equivalence point , the mol of H ⁺ from the acid
the mol of
ion produced by the base.
Amount of H ⁺ ion in flask = amount of
ion added
The end point occurs when there is a slight excess of base and the indicator changes color permanently.

An Acid-base Titration
Figure 4.14
Source: Stephen Frisch/McGraw Hill

Oxidation-Reduction (Redox) Reactions
Oxidation is the loss of electrons and reduction is the gain of electrons.
The oxidizing agent gains electrons and is reduced. And the reducing agent loses electrons and is oxidized.
A redox reaction involves electron transfer .
Oxidation and reduction occur together.

The Redox Process in Compound Formation
Figure 4.15
Access the text alternative for slide images. [slide123.xml]

Table 4.6: Rules for Assigning an Oxidation Number (O.N.)
For an atom in its elemental form (Na, C, O 2 , Cl 2 , P 4 , etc.): O.N. = 0.
For a monatomic ion: O.N. = ion charge (with the sign before the numeral).
The sum of O.N. values for the atoms in a molecule or formula unit of a compound equals zero. The sum of O.N. values for the atoms in a polyatomic ion equals the ion’s charge.
The table summarizes the rules involved for assigning an oxidation number for an atom. For an atom in elemental form oxidation number is zero, in monoatomic ion oxidation number equals ion charge (with the sign before the numeral). For polyatomic ion, the sum of their ionic charges are considered. For groups in periodic table, oxidation number is discussed for metals and non-metals.
Rules for Specific Atoms or Periodic Table Groups
1. For Group 1: | O.N. = +1 in all compounds
2. For Group 2: | O.N. = +2 in all compounds
3. For hydrogen: | O.N. = +1 in combination with nonmetals 
O.N. = −1 in combination with metals and boron 
(for example, N a H)
4. For fluorine: | O.N. = −1 in all compounds
5. For oxygen: | O.N. = −1 in peroxides (for example, H2O2)
O.N. = −2 in all other compounds (except with F)
6. For Group 17: | O.N. = −1 in combination with metals, nonmetals (except O), and other halogens lower in the group

Sample Problem 4.15: Problem and Plan
Determining the Oxidation Number of Each Element in a Compound (or Ion)
PROBLEM: Determine the oxidation number (O.N.) of each element in these species:
(a) zinc chloride (b) sulfur trioxide (c) nitric acid (d) dichromate ion
PLAN: Use Table 4.6 to assign each atom an O.N.

Sample Problem 4.15: Solution
(a) Z n C l 2 . The O.N. of each chloride ion is −1 for a total of −2, so the O.N. of Z n must be +2 since the sum of O.N.s must equal zero for a compound.
(b) S O 3 . The O.N. of each oxygen is −2 for a total of −6. The O.N.s must add up to zero, so the O.N. of S is +6.
(c) H N O 3 . The O.N. of H is +1 and the O.N. of each O is −2 for a total of −6. Therefore, the O.N. of N is +5.
(d)
The sum of the O.N. values in a polyatomic ion
equals the ion’s charge. The O.N. of each O is −2, so the total for seven O atoms is −14. Therefore, each Cr must have an O.N. of +6 in order for the sum of the O.N.s to equal the charge of the ion.

Sample Problem 4.16: Problem and Plan
Identifying Redox Reactions and Oxidizing and Reducing Agents
PROBLEM: Use oxidation numbers to decide which of the following are redox reactions. For each redox reaction, identify the oxidizing agent and the reducing agent.
(a)
(b)
(c)
PLAN: Use Table 4.6 to assign an O. N. to each atom and then note whether it changes as the reactants become products. A reactant is the reducing agent if it contains an atom that is oxidized, while a reactant is the oxidizing agent if it contains an atom that is reduced.

Sample Problem 4.16: Solution 1
SOLUTION:
0          +1 +6  − 2            +3 +6  −2           0
Since the O.N. of Al and H change, this is a redox reaction. The O.N. of Al changes from 0 to +3 (Al loses electrons), so A l is oxidized and Al is the reducing agent. The O.N. of H ⁺ decreases from +1 to 0 (H gains electrons), so H is reduced and H 2 S O 4 is the oxidizing agent.
+ 1 +6 − 2      +1  −2 +1         +1 +6  −2       +1  − 2
Each atom in the products has the same O.N. that it had in the reactants; this is not a redox reaction.

Sample Problem 4.16: Solution 2
SOLUTION:
+ 2  −2   +2  − 2       0         +4  −2
Since the O.N. of Pb and C change, this is a redox reaction. The O.N. of C changes from +2 to +4 (C loses electrons), so C is oxidized and C O is the reducing agent. The O.N. of P b decreases from +2 to 0 (P b gains electrons), so P b is reduced and P b O is the oxidizing agent.

Elements in Redox Reactions
Combination Reactions:
Two or more reactants combine to form a new compound:
X + Y → Z.
Decomposition Reactions:
A single compound decomposes to form two or more products:
Z → X + Y.
Displacement Reactions:
D ouble displacement: A B + CD → AD + CB.
Single displacement: X + YZ → XZ + Y.
Combustion:
The process of combining with O 2.

Sample Problem 4.18: Problem and Plan
Identifying the Type of Redox Reaction
PROBLEM: Classify each of the following redox reactions as a combination, decomposition, or displacement reaction. Write a balanced molecular equation for each, as well as total and net ionic equations for part (c), and identify the oxidizing and reducing agents:
(a)
(b)
(c)
PLAN: Combination reactions have fewer products than reactants, decomposition reactions have more products than reactants, and displacement reactions have the same number of reactants and products.

Sample Problem 4.18:  Solution
SOLUTION: (a) This is a combination reaction, since Mg and N 2 combine. Mg is the reducing agent;  N 2 is the oxidizing agent.
(b) This is a decomposition reaction, since H 2 O 2 breaks down. H 2 O 2 is both the reducing and the oxidizing agent.
(c) This is a displacement reaction, since Al displaces
from solution. Al
is the reducing agent;
is the oxidizing agent.

Mass-mole-number-volume Relationships in Solution - Text Alternative
Return to parent-slide containing images. [slide7.xml]
The steps involved in the flowchart are as follows: amount of substance in solution in moles leads to mass of substance in solution in grams with number of moles times molar mass; mass of substance in solution in grams leads to amount of substance in solution in moles with mass by molar mass; amount of substance in solution in moles leads to entities of substance in solution with number of moles times Avogadro’s number; entities of substance in solution lead to amount of substance in solution in moles with number of entities over Avogadro’s number; amount of substance in solution in moles leads to volume of solution in liters with number of moles over molarity; volume of solution in liters leads to amount of substance in solution in moles with molarity times volume.
Return to parent-slide containing images. [slide7.xml]

The Electrical Conductivity of Ionic Solutions - Text Alternative
Return to parent-slide containing images. [slide19.xml]
The lightbulb in distilled water does not light because there are no ions in solution and therefore no charge carriers to conduct electricity. The lightbulb in an ionic solid does not light because the charge associated with the ions is fixed and cannot move. The lightbulb in ionic solution lights up due to movement of ions and therefore electricity.
Return to parent-slide containing images. [slide19.xml]

Amount-mass-number Relationships - Text Alternative
Return to parent-slide containing images. [slide47.xml]
The steps involved in the flowchart are as follows: amount of substance A in moles leads to mass of substance A in grams with number of moles times molar mass; mass of substance A in grams leads to amount of substance A in moles with mass by molar mass; amount of substance A in moles leads to volume of solution of A in liters with number of moles over molarity; volume of solution of A in liters leads to amount of substance A in moles with molarity times volume; amount of substance A in moles leads to entities such as atoms, molecules, or formula units, of substance A with number of moles times Avogadro’s number; entities of substance A lead to amount of substance A in moles with number of entities over Avogadro’s number; amount of substance B in moles leads to mass of substance B in grams with number of moles times molar mass; mass of substance B in grams leads to amount of substance B in moles with mass by molar mass; amount of substance B in moles leads to volume of solution of B in liters with number of moles over molarity; volume of solution of B in liters leads to amount of substance B in moles with molarity times volume; amount of substance B in moles leads to entities such as atoms, molecules, or formula units, of substance B with number of moles times Avogadro’s number; entities of substance B lead to amount of substance B in moles with number of entities over Avogadro’s number; amount of substance A in moles is in equilibrium with amount of substance B in moles with molar ratio from balanced equation.
Return to parent-slide containing images. [slide47.xml]

Sample Problem 4.10: Plan - Text Alternative
Return to parent-slide containing images. [slide49.xml]
Volume F e C l 3 in liters is converted to the amount in moles by multiplying by the molarity (1 liter solution equals 0.250 moles F e C l 3 ). This is converted to the amount in moles of F e(O H) 3 by the molar ratio. The amount of N a O H in liters is converted to the moles N a O H by multiplying by the molarity of solution (1 liter solution equals 0.300 moles N a O H). This is converted to the amount in moles of F e(O H) 3 by the molar ratio. The resulting moles of F e(O H) 3 are compared and the lowest value is multiplied by the molar mass to yield the grams of F e(O H) 3 .

Sample Problem 4.11: Problem and Plan - Text Alternative
Return to parent-slide containing images. [slide59.xml]
The volume of H N O 3 in milliliters is converted to liters by the following relationship (1000 milliliters equals 1 liter). This is converted to the amount H N O 3 in moles by multiplying by the molarity (1 liter solution equals 1.4 moles H N O 3 ). This is converted to the moles of H+ ions by the relationship between the two species (moles H+ divided by the moles H N O 3 ). Finally this is converted to the number of H+ ions by multiplying by Avogadro's number.

Proton Transfer Process - Text Alternative
Return to parent-slide containing images. [slide62.xml]
Strong acid, H X, shows H plus ion depicted by a white sphere and X minus ion depicted by a green sphere among water molecules. Strong base, M O H, shows M plus ion depicted by a gray sphere and O H minus ion depicted by a red sphere bonded to a white sphere among water molecules. Oxygen atom is depicted by the red sphere, and the hydrogen atom is depicted by the white sphere. Aqueous solutions of strong acid and strong base are mixed resulting in a solution containing M plus ion, X minus ion, and H 2 O molecule. Chemical change involving transfer of H plus ion to O H minus ion results in the formation of H 2 O molecule. M plus ion and X minus ions remain in solution as spectator ions. In the presence of heat, evaporation of water leaves solid salt, M X. The salt crystal is depicted by M plus and X minus ions organized in a lattice structure. Few water molecules move freely and disorganized. Accompanying ionic equation reads, H plus in aqueous state plus X minus in aqueous state plus M plus in aqueous state plus O H minus in aqueous state when mixed yield the intermediates, H 2 O in liquid state plus M plus in aqueous state plus X minus in aqueous state. In the presence of heat, the intermediates yield H 2 O in gaseous state plus M X in solid state.
Return to parent-slide containing images. [slide62.xml]

A Gas-forming Reaction with a Weak Acid - Text Alternative
Return to parent-slide containing images. [slide65.xml]
The addition leads to bubbling of the solution in the beaker. The three types of equation are molecular equation, total ionic equation, and net ionic equation. Molecular equation reads, N a H C O 3 in aqueous state plus H C 2 H 3 O 2 in aqueous state yield N a C 2 H 3 O 2 in aqueous state plus C O 2 in gaseous state plus H 2 O in liquid state. Total ionic equation reads, N a plus in aqueous state plus H C O 3 minus in aqueous state plus H C 2 H 3 O 2 in aqueous state yield C 2 H 3 O 2 minus in aqueous state plus N a plus in aqueous state plus C O 2 in gaseous state plus H 2 O in liquid state. Net ionic equation reads, H C O 3 minus in aqueous state plus H C 2 H 3 O 2 in aqueous state yield C 2 H 3 O 2 minus in aqueous state plus C O 2 in gaseous state plus H 2 O in liquid state.
Return to parent-slide containing images. [slide65.xml]

Summary of Terminology for Redox Reactions - Text Alternative
Return to parent-slide containing images. [slide85.xml]
In the transfer of electrons from X to Y, X loses electrons, is oxidized, is the reducing agent and increases its oxidation number. Y gains electrons, is reduced, is the oxidizing agent and decreases its oxidation number.
Return to parent-slide containing images. [slide85.xml]

Decomposition of Mercury(II) Oxide - Text Alternative
Return to parent-slide containing images. [slide92.xml]
In H g O, the Hg ion has a +2 formal charge and the O ion has a − 2 formal charge. When these split during the reaction shown, elemental Hg and elemental O are both neutral.

Lithium Displaces H 2 from Water - Text Alternative
Return to parent-slide containing images. [slide93.xml]
In metallic L i , L i atoms are neutral. In H 2 O, the O has a formal charge of − 2 and each H has a formal charge of +1. The product L i O H( aq ) results in a formal charge of +1 for L i , −2 for O, and +1 for H. The product H 2 is neutral.
Return to parent-slide containing images. [slide93.xml]

Copper Displacement of Silver - Text Alternative
Return to parent-slide containing images. [slide95.xml]
A copper wire is placed in a solution of A g N O 3 ( aq ). C u atoms are stripped off the wire to bond with N O 3 ions. Ag atoms are simultaneously deposited onto the wire. The resulting solution contains C u(N O 3 ) 2 , which has a blue color. Ag is reduced from +1 to 0, and C u is oxidized from 0 to +2.
Return to parent-slide containing images. [slide95.xml]

The Activity Series of the Halogens - Text Alternative
Return to parent-slide containing images. [slide97.xml]
The reactivity is as follows: Iodine, atomic number, 53, atomic mass, 126.9 atomic mass unit: Iodine molecule is the least reactive halogen and cannot oxidize any halide ions above it. Bromine, atomic number, 35, atomic mass, 79.90 atomic mass unit: Bromine molecule can oxidize only iodide ion. Chlorine, atomic number, 17, atomic mass, 35.45 atomic mass unit: Chlorine molecule can oxidize bromide and iodide ions. Fluorine, atomic number, 9, atomic mass, 19.00 atomic mass unit: Fluorine molecule is the most reactive halogen and can displace, oxidize, chloride, bromide, and iodide ions.
Return to parent-slide containing images. [slide97.xml]

The Equilibrium State - Text Alternative
Return to parent-slide containing images. [slide100.xml]
When C a C O 3 is heated in an open container, the reaction goes to completion because C O 2 escapes. This is an example of a non-equilibrium system. When C a C O 3 is heated in a closed container, the reaction reaches equilibrium because all substances are present. The result is a mixture of C a O and C a C O 3 .
Return to parent-slide containing images. [slide100.xml]
